preFix.title: Setting up an outdoor pig paddock from scratch
preFix.subtitle: Electric fencing, shelter, water, and the sequence of steps before the weaners arrive.
preFix.excerpt: Pigs require electric fencing that they have been trained to respect, a dry weatherproof shelter, clean water at all times, and a holding that meets welfare regulations. Getting this right before the pigs arrive is far easier than fixing problems while managing two curious, strong weaners.
preFix.sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Pigs, 2003. AHDB Pork, Outdoor Pig Setup guidance. Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living, pig chapters.

content.text: The setup for outdoor pigs takes a day to complete but must be right before the weaners arrive. A poorly positioned arc, an untested fence, or a water supply needing daily topping-up all become headaches the moment the pigs are in the paddock.

Before any pig arrives, ensure you have a CPH number from the Rural Payments Agency, are registered with APHA as a pig keeper, and have an eAML2 account. These take a few days to arrange. Do not accept pigs until the paperwork is in place; the movement document requires your CPH before the seller can complete it.

on the driest, highest-drained ground in the paddock. The arc entrance should face broadly south, away from the prevailing wind. Bed it with a 15 cm base of straw before the pigs arrive; they will organise the bedding themselves once in. If the ground is soft clay, position the arc on a base of hardcore or gravel if possible.
Run the fence at two heights: nose level (20-25 cm) and shoulder height (35-40 cm). Test with a fence tester before the pigs arrive; minimum 3,000 volts in the field for reliable deterrence.
in a small holding pen for 24 hours before releasing into the full paddock makes the difference between pigs that respect the fence for the whole season and pigs that push through it on day one.

Pigs drink 5-8 litres of water per day, more in summer. A ball-valve water trough plumbed to a mains supply is far more reliable than a manually filled container. Position it on firmer ground or a concrete block to keep the area around the trough from turning into a mud wallow (pigs will stand in any water container given the chance). Check daily regardless.

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
